title: Paste paper with wheat paste
subtitle: Textured decorative paper using combed wheat paste and pigment
excerpt: Paste paper is a European decorative-paper tradition in which cooked wheat paste coloured with pigment is spread onto dampened paper and combed or textured before drying. The technique predates carrageenan marbling and was used for bookbinding endpapers and covers from the seventeenth century. No float bath is required.
sourceNotes: C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853, 1881), discusses paste-paper as a precursor and companion technique to water-bath marbling. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/artofmarbling00wool. The wheat-paste formula described here follows the standard historical practice documented in multiple bookbinding sources of the period.

projectSchedule.title: Cook paste, colour, and texture the paper
projectSchedule.body: Stretch the paper, cook the wheat paste, add pigment, brush onto the dampened stretched sheet, and comb into patterns before the paste sets.

projectSchedule.title: Remove from boards and press flat
projectSchedule.body: Peel the dried paste-paper sheet from its board and press under heavy books for two hours to flatten.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked starch paste made from plain wheat flour and water. When cooled, it forms a smooth, spreadable vehicle for paste-paper pigment and an adhesive used in bookbinding and conservation.

Decorative paper made by applying coloured wheat paste to dampened paper and texturing it with combs, fingers, or other tools before drying. A European bookbinding tradition from the seventeenth century.

content.text: is simpler than water-bath marbling: it requires no float bath, no mordanted paper, and no ox gall. A cooked

is coloured with acrylic or watercolour pigment, brushed over a dampened sheet, and textured with whatever tools are to hand: a comb, a finger, a crumpled piece of cling film. The result dries stiff and colourful, with a surface that takes gilding, embossing, or further painting.

items.name: Acrylic paint or watercolour for colouring the paste
Heavy paper or cartridge paper, 120 gsm or heavier
Stiff board or sheet of glass for stretching the paper
items.qty: 1 per sheet, larger than the paper

items.name: Gum tape (water-activated) for stretching the paper flat
items.qty: Enough for all four edges of each sheet

items.name: Wide brush or foam roller for applying paste
Combs: hair comb, fork, or card-notched comb

content.text: Dampened paste-paper sheets cockle badly unless stretched flat before the paste is applied. Sponge both faces of each sheet with clean water until evenly damp but not soaking. Lay the sheet on a board and tape all four edges down with gum tape. The sheet will cockle slightly as you tape; it will pull flat as it dries. Allow five minutes for the sheet to settle before applying paste.

Mix two tablespoons of plain flour into 250 ml of cold water until there are no lumps. Heat over a low flame, stirring constantly, until the mixture thickens to the consistency of a thin white sauce. This takes four to six minutes. Remove from heat and stir for a further two minutes. The paste should be smooth and slightly translucent, and should coat a spoon. Allow to cool to room temperature before adding pigment.

Stir acrylic paint into the cooled paste: start with a teaspoon and stir well. The paste should be strongly coloured, as it lightens slightly when dry. Brush or roll the coloured paste over the dampened stretched sheet in a thin, even coat. Then work the paste immediately while still wet: draw a comb through it in straight stripes, wavy lines, or spirals. Crumple a piece of cling film and press it into the paste for a crackle texture. Work quickly because the paste begins to set as the paper absorbs moisture and stops being workable after three to five minutes.

Leave the paste-paper sheet taped to the board until fully dry, usually overnight. The texture firms as it dries. Peel the gum-tape edges carefully when dry and lift the sheet from the board. It will be noticeably stiffer than before. Press the finished sheet under heavy books for two hours to flatten it before use as endpaper or a book cover.

items.symptom: The paste surface has a rough, grainy texture when dry
items.cause: The flour was not fully cooked, leaving starch granules in the paste.
items.fix: Cook the paste for at least four minutes over medium heat with constant stirring. The paste should become completely smooth and slightly translucent before removing from heat. Undercooking is the most common failure.

items.symptom: The sheet cockles and warps badly after drying despite being taped
items.cause: The paper weight is too low for the paste moisture, or the paste coat was too thick.
items.fix: Use heavier paper, minimum 120 gsm. Apply a thinner coat of paste and spread it further. A thinner coat also produces finer texture detail.
